prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. This is for her college application — it really matters. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

- Does it address what was learned? Yes - multiple lessons articulated
- Is it a personal narrative? Yes, it's written in first person and tells a story

**What Works Well:**

1. **Authentic voice and tone** - The writing feels genuine and age-appropriate. Phrases like "sounds like a weird pivot" and "So yeah" feel natural, not forced.

2. **Clear narrative arc** - Has a beginning (overconfidence), middle (realization and failure), and end (reflection and moving forward).

3. **Specific details** - "pick and roll," the kid saying "stay out of the way," Marcus making the team - these concrete details make it vivid.

# Feedback: 8/10

## What Works Well

**Authentic voice** — This genuinely sounds like a real person, not someone trying too hard. Phrases like "sounds like a weird pivot" feel natural.

**Smart structure** — Clear arc from overconfidence → humbling reality → redirection → reflection. The debate pivot is particularly effective because it shows adaptability, not just bounce-back resilience.

**Mature reflection** — The final two paragraphs are genuinely thoughtful. The progression from "failure sucks" to "failure redirected me" to "the real lesson is about what I didn't know I didn't know" shows real intellectual growth.

**Specific details** — "pick and roll," Marcus making the team, the kid saying "stay out of the way" — these make it memorable rather than generic.

## What Could Strengthen It

**More emotional texture in the middle** — You tell us "I was really upset for a while" but don't *show* us much of what that felt like. The scrimmage scene (which is the most vulnerable moment) moves through it quickly. Lean into one specific moment with more sensory detail or emotional honesty.

**The Marcus dynamic feels underdeveloped** — He's mentioned twice but his presence is a bit murky. Did this hurt your friendship? Did you resent him? A clearer picture of that relationship would add depth.

**Slightly over-explained ending** — By the last paragraphs, you've already *shown* that casual skill ≠ competitive skill. Spelling it out explicitly ("being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean...") feels a bit like telling rather than trusting the reader.

**Debate deserves more** — You mention debate was better, but one concrete example of what made you good at it (or how it differed from basketball) would strengthen the "better fit" claim.

## Bottom Line

This is a genuinely solid essay with real self-awareness and an authentic voice. It shows you can fail, reflect honestly, and adapt. For a college app, that's exactly what admissions wants. It's not the most vulnerable or surprising essay, but it's well-executed. Your friend should feel good about this.

# Grade: 7/10

## What Works Well

**Strong narrative clarity.** This essay has a clear arc with specific, concrete details (the "pick and roll," the scrimmage, that kid telling her to stay out of the way). You can visualize the scenes.

**Genuine voice.** It reads like an actual teenager reflecting, not someone trying too hard to sound profound. That authenticity is valuable.

**Real vulnerability.** She admits she was wrong about her skill level and shows actual disappointment rather than pretending it didn't matter.

**Meaningful pivot.** The debate tangent isn't random—it shows adaptability and reveals something about her strengths. Good instinct.

**Solid reflective closure.** "You don't know what you don't know" is actually a strong insight earned through the narrative.

## What Holds It Back

**It's predictable.** The failure-leads-to-better-path arc is extremely common in college essays. Many admissions officers will have read versions of this.

**Surface-level introspection.** The essay recounts events more than it explores the *internal experience* of failing. What did she tell herself? How did she rationalize it? What specific moment changed her mind about trying again?

**The learning feels slightly generic.** Phrases like "failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information" sound a bit like she's wrapping up neatly rather than wrestling with something real.

**Underdeveloped elements.** Why debate specifically? What about it clicked? The Marcus comparison could cut deeper—what did it feel like to see him succeed?

## Bottom Line

This is a **competent, honest essay** that fulfills the assignment. It shows self-awareness and growth. But it doesn't quite have the specificity, emotional complexity, or fresh insight that makes essays memorable to admissions officers. It's solidly in the "good" category but not the "exceptional" category.

**Suggestion:** Have her consider digging into one moment—maybe the scrimmage or the day the list went up—and really *showing* the emotional struggle rather than summarizing it.

1. **Clear narrative structure**: The essay has a logical flow - setup, attempt, failure, reaction, reflection, lessons learned. It's easy to follow.

3. **Honest voice**: The tone feels genuine and age-appropriate for a high school student. There's no pretentiousness or over-reaching attempts to sound mature.

4. **Genuine self-reflection**: The writer doesn't just say "I learned to try harder." There are real insights about the difference between casual and competitive play, and how failure led to a better path.

6. **Relatable**: Most readers have had a similar experience of overestimating their abilities.

# Feedback: 7.5/10 (B+)

## What Works Really Well

**Specific details & honesty**: The "pick and roll," the scrimmage scene, "just stay out of the way" — these ground the story and feel genuine. No fake drama or overwrought emotion.

**Clear voice**: This reads like a real person, not someone trying to sound impressive. That matters for college essays.

**Multiple layers of learning**: You don't just get "I learned to try harder." The insights about driveway vs. competitive play, and the broader "you don't know what you don't know" are thoughtful.

**Good narrative arc**: Setup → failure → reflection → growth. It works.

## What Could Be Stronger

**The reflection part moves too fast**: The basketball failure gets good scene-setting, but once you pivot to debate, it gets rushed. You mention debate was good for you, but *show* us more. What was the first debate experience? A specific moment where you realized you were better at this?

**The ending is a bit generic**: "Failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information" — true, but it's the kind of thing many essays say. Can you make this more specific to *your* story? What does this failure specifically teach that someone else's failure might not?

**Unexplored thread — Marcus**: He made the team and you didn't. That's genuinely hard. Did you talk about it? How did that affect your friendship? This could add emotional weight.

**Pacing**: The emotional recovery feels glossed over ("I was really upset for a while"). A bit more detail here could deepen things.

## Bottom Line

This is **genuinely good** — it's honest, specific, and shows real growth. A college admissions officer would get a clear sense of you as a person. It just has room to go from "solid" to "memorable" by deepening one or two moments.

**Suggested edit**: Develop the debate part and/or the Marcus dynamic more. Cut something else if needed.

## What Works Really Well

- **Specific and honest**: The concrete details (Marcus, "pick and roll," the kid telling them to stay out of the way) make this feel real, not manufactured
- **Genuine growth arc**: This doesn't feel like you're checking boxes. The student was actually overconfident, genuinely struggled, and learned something real
- **Mature reflection**: The final insights go beyond "I learned to try harder"—they articulate something deeper about the gap between casual competence and real skill
- **Authentic voice**: The tone is conversational without being unprofessional. It sounds like a real person, not an essay-writing robot
- **Shows adaptation, not just resilience**: Pivoting to debate instead of just trying harder at basketball shows flexibility and self-knowledge

## Room for Improvement

- **The debate pivot could use more development**: Why debate specifically? What about it clicked? Even 1-2 more sentences would strengthen this
- **Could dig slightly deeper into reflection**: "debate ended up being one of the best experiences" is good, but showing *how* debate developed her (confidence? public speaking? quick thinking?) would be stronger
- **Minor tone note**: "So yeah" and "Obviously" are fine for authenticity, but a touch more polish wouldn't hurt a college essay

## Bottom Line

This is a strong personal narrative that does exactly what colleges want to see: shows self-awareness, genuine growth, and wisdom earned through actual experience. It's honest without being defensive, specific without being petty. Your friend should feel good about this.

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

# Grade: 8/10

## What Works

**Strong voice and authenticity** — This reads like a real person reflecting genuinely, not someone trying to impress. The casual honesty ("Obviously. Marcus made it though") feels earned.

**Good structure** — Clear arc from setup through failure to reflection. Specific details (pick and roll, "stay out of the way," driveway vs. competitive play) make it concrete.

**Real self-awareness** — The essay understands the difference between casual competence and actual skill, and shows growth beyond just "I learned I'm resilient."

**Natural lesson** — Doesn't oversell the significance or try to make this seem like a life-altering moment.

**The failure itself is somewhat common** — Not making a sports team is relatable, which is good, but it's also a well-worn topic. A lot of applicants will write about this. Nothing wrong with that, but it won't stand out.

**The insight stays surface-level** — "Failure is just information" and "you don't know what you don't know" are genuine observations, but they're also fairly predictable takeaways. Dig deeper. What specifically changed about how you see yourself?

**The debate pivot is underdeveloped** — This is actually the most interesting part of the story (how failure redirected you to something better-suited), but it gets mentioned and dropped. Expand this section—it's where the real story is.

**Emotional vulnerability could be stronger** — We know she was upset, but show us more. What did disappointment actually *feel* like?

## Bottom Line

This is well-written and demonstrates good reflection. For a strong college, it's solid but not distinctive—your friend might get lost in a pile of similar sports-failure essays. The best revision would be to deepen the exploration of either the failure itself or (better) the debate discovery and what that taught her about herself.

# Feedback: 8/10

**What Works:**

- **Strong narrative structure** — Clear arc with specific moments (driveway games, the scrimmage, the rejection, the pivot to debate)
- **Genuine honesty** — The vulnerability feels real; you don't hide the sting or your initial self-deception about your level
- **Authentic reflection** — The lessons aren't preachy; they emerge naturally from the story
- **Good specific details** — "pick and roll," Marcus making the team, that kid saying "stay out of the way" — these ground the essay
- **The pivot matters** — Moving to debate shows this failure actually *changed her trajectory* rather than just being a sad story

**What Needs Work:**

- **The prose is a bit plain** — Sentences are straightforward and serviceable, but lack vivid detail. What did the gym *feel* like during tryouts? What did she actually *see* when she realized she was outmatched?
- **Some telling instead of showing** — "I was really upset for a while" is summary. Show us that upset through a specific moment or image
- **Clichéd phrasing** — "out of my league," "failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information" — these ideas are good but expressed in predictable ways
- **Underdeveloped ending** — The debate team deserves more. Why was she naturally good at it? How did it connect to strengths she discovered? This is the most interesting part of her story
- **Tone occasionally off** — "Obviously" at "When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously." feels a bit dismissive

**To Strengthen It:**
Revise to add 1-2 vivid scenes (maybe one sensory-rich moment from tryouts?) and develop the debate payoff more fully. That's where the real growth is, and it deserves space.

This is a solid, honest essay that will serve her well.

prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. This is for her college application — it really matters. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

What was different about it? This could be the most distinctive part of your essay, so give it room to breathe.

**Go deeper on the emotions.** "I was really upset for a while" and the Marcus comparison hint at real, complicated feelings. Lean into that rather than summarizing it.

This is a solid, honest essay that does the job. It shows self-awareness and genuine growth. For a college essay, it's in the "good" range—it won't hurt your application. But it plays it a bit safe in places where you could be more specific and vulnerable. The debate pivot is your most unique element; let *that* be the centerpiece rather than the footnote.

# Grade: 8/10

## What Works Really Well

**Authentic voice and honesty** — Your friend doesn't make excuses or try to seem noble. Admitting "I barely touched the ball" and that the kid wasn't wrong about her being in over her head shows maturity and self-awareness that colleges notice.

**Specific details** — "Pick and roll," Marcus, the scrimmage moment, the pivot to debate. These make it real instead of generic.

**Actual growth with evidence** — The essay doesn't just say "I learned failure is okay." It shows how she adapted by finding something she was actually good at. That's compelling.

**Clear narrative arc** — Setup → failure → reflection → growth. Easy to follow, well-paced.

## Where It Could Strengthen

**The emotional depth could go deeper** — What did the scrimmage actually *feel* like? The embarrassment, the self-doubt in your body? Right now the failure feels reported rather than experienced.

**The debate pivot feels slightly rushed** — "I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me" glosses over what could be an interesting moment. Why debate specifically? What was that transition like?

**The ending is a bit generic** — "Failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information" sounds wise but reads like something everyone learns. What's the *specific* way your friend thinks about failure now that's uniquely hers?

**Minor tone note** — "Obviously. Marcus made it though" — the one-word sentence feels slightly flippant for a college essay.

## Bottom Line
This is a solid, honest essay that shows real self-awareness and growth. It's well-written and will serve her fine. To make it exceptional, she'd need to go deeper into the *feeling* of failure and what makes her reflection uniquely hers.
